article_title: Self-Propagating JavaScript Worm Vandalized Nearly 4,000 Wikipedia Pages in 23 Minutes Before Engineers Contained the Spread

content_preview.title: Self-Propagating JavaScript Worm Vandalized Nearly 4,000 Wikipedia Pages in 23 Minutes Before Engineers Contained the Spread
content_preview.summary: A dormant malicious script planted on Russian Wikipedia in 2024 was inadvertently activated during a Wikimedia security review, modifying thousands of pages and 85 user scripts before engineers locked down editing across all projects.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## Overview

On March 5, 2026, a self-propagating JavaScript worm tore through Wikipedia and other Wikimedia projects, modifying approximately 3,996 pages and replacing around 85 user script files in a 23-minute window before engineers contained the outbreak. The incident, which the Wikimedia Foundation has formally designated the [March 2026 User Script Incident](https://meta.wikimedia.org/wiki/Wikimedia_Foundation/Product_and_Technology/Product_Safety_and_Integrity/March_2026_User_Script_Incid...

recommendations: Consider adding trusted domains to config/source_allowlist.txt

editor_notes.content_quality: Well-written News article at 649 words covering the Wikipedia JS worm incident. Clear technical explanation of the propagation mechanism. Good structure with impact, response, unknowns, and architectural context sections.
editor_notes.source_verification: 3 sources: BleepingComputer (primary news report, on allowlist), Wikimedia Foundation official incident report on Meta-Wiki (primary source), SecureBlink (secondary cybersecurity outlet). BleepingComputer provides detailed technical analysis; Wikimedia provides the official Foundation statement.
editor_notes.factual_accuracy: Verified against source snapshots: 3,996 pages modified and ~85 user common.js files replaced (BleepingComputer), 23-minute active window (BleepingComputer + Wikimedia incident report), User:Ololoshka562/test.js on Russian Wikipedia first uploaded March 2024 (BleepingComputer), self-propagation via common.js and MediaWiki:Common.js injection (BleepingComputer), Special:Random used for page selection (BleepingComputer), dormant code activated during security review (Wikimedia official statement), Meta-Wiki bore brunt of deletions (BleepingComputer + Wikimedia), editing temporarily restricted across all projects (Wikimedia). Article correctly notes uncertainty about whether activation was accidental, automated, or from compromised account — matching BleepingComputer's reporting.
editor_notes.tone_assessment: Neutral and technical. No sensationalism. Appropriately balanced between describing the incident's impact and noting it caused no permanent damage or data breach.
editor_notes.originality: No existing articles on the March 2026 Wikipedia worm incident. Completely distinct topic.
recommendations: meta.wikimedia.org should be added to source allowlist as an official Wikimedia Foundation source
editor_notes.overall_assessment: Excellent incident report with strong primary sourcing from both BleepingComputer's technical analysis and the Wikimedia Foundation's official incident report. All key claims verified. Ready for publication.
